title: How to Do It Now Because It’s Not Going Away

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
There is no doubt that everyone procrastinates in one way or the other. Nowadays, kids take so much comfort in the art of procrastination that they tend to forget how harmful it can be to their growth and development in life. Overcoming procrastination isn’t something to be taken lightly. The strategies in this summary to fight procrastination aren’t ones you can absorb all at once — a little at a time implementation.

Focus on one aspect of your life that you want to improve, and then work your way up.

When you try to implement several strategies simultaneously, it compounds the procrastination problem.

A 2014 study shows that 80 to 95% of college students regularly procrastinate, particularly when completing assignments and coursework.

Learning to overcome procrastination can make the difference between a successful life and an average life. In fact, it is often the seemingly invisible things in our lives that most affect us. Thus, bringing them to the light and course-correcting where necessary will lead to a meaningful life.

Procrastination seems easy. “I’ll just do it later when I have the time,” but what you fail to understand is that it makes you feel stressed and anxious. Consequently, it will negatively affect your grades. So why then is it hard for you to stay motivated and focused in getting that work done? Why do you have to wait for the consequences you are to suffer? All our answers still come down to procrastination, and this summary shares some insights on how to get rid of it and start maximizing your time to achieve better results.

As an academic/life coach for teens and college students for the past fifteen years, not a day goes by where I am not coaching, emailing, or working with a student on overcoming their procrastination. ~ Leslie Josel

chapters.title: People procrastinate for different reasons, and not all of them are bad
chapters.text:
The act of procrastinating is multilayered and complex. People often believe it is simple and easy and revolves around “just being lazy.” But when it comes to procrastination, there is more than meets the eye. People often procrastinate for different reasons but putting things off does not necessarily make them lazy.

Managing your time effectively with the right tools and strategies will help you overcome procrastination.

You can’t get over something if you don’t know or have an idea why it is happening. So, to manage procrastination is to understand why you do it, to begin with.

Some procrastinate because they don’t have an organized workspace. Others do it because they want everything to be perfect. Lack of proper planning or scheduling causes some to put off things they need to do. Distractions also rank high in the list of reasons for procrastination. Mood swings, the lack of routine and discipline required to stay on task, indecision, and having too much to do are other popular reasons we procrastinate.

Instead of getting things done, you often find yourself making excuses like: “I have everything under control.” These excuses turn into a mantra and hinder your productivity level. But managing your time effectively and efficiently will combat this problem.

Learning to see time in more tangible ways can help take its invisibility out of the equation. ~ Leslie Josel

Effective time management should not just be any skill but a life skill that you include in all your activities. To manage your time, you have to be well aware of how long it takes to get things done, create a time estimate, and work towards it. That way, you have no problem setting limits for yourself and planning toward achieving certain goals.

chapters.title: Making excuses can become a habit, and it needs to be checked
chapters.text:
Nobody enjoys too much work, especially homework, because it sometimes takes a while to complete. As a result, you begin to come up with different excuses that cause you to procrastinate doing it. Excuses lead to complaining, which in turn leads to procrastination. When trying to get things done, you have to stop complaining and start doing. Is homework hard for you to do? Find the best way to control that hardship and complete your tasks on time. When you stop procrastinating, you get more work done with less stress.

Build up a schedule that you can work with. Managing your time requires a certain amount of dedication. The first step you can take is to write down your tasks and estimate the time frame for completing them before embarking on them. As time goes on, you can improve on your time estimates and get better at finishing your tasks on time.

In managing your time and avoiding procrastination, use the time horizon — it is how far you can look into the future and plan ahead.

Planning is a very crucial aspect of time management. A lack of plans makes it difficult to get things done on time. Ultimately, you start to fall behind schedule, making accomplishing certain things impossible.

Practice the 2-minute rule. The mantra states that you should do a task immediately if it takes less than two minutes to complete. Instead of filling your mind with a long list of small to-dos, you get in the habit of doing them the minute they appear.

Tentatively, in fighting procrastination, find a comfortable worksite, set timers, and attend to tasks the minute you receive them. That way, the motivation to work will increase, and you’ll find yourself achieving your goals without having to defer them to a future date.

chapters.title: Taking breaks is vital for productivity, but there is a catch to doing it right
chapters.text:
Working for long hours without breaks might not be as productive as expected. That is why instead of procrastinating that work into the future and getting it done all in one night, it is better to incorporate breaks into the work and spread it over a period of time. There are seven steps to take to achieve this goal:

• Set a timer for work.
• Stick to the plan.
• Stay away from social media while working because it can be a significant distractor.
• Step away from studying and go for a walk to clear your head.
• Stretch your muscles and relax.
• Once in a while, switch your study location.
• Snack on something. And then repeat.

Pulling an all-nighter or cramming a few hours before an exam puts pressure on your brain.

Spread your studying hours throughout the day. Our ability to recall information and theories is more pronounced if we learn in multiple spread-out sessions. Employ some easy to work with tips that will help you study effectively and increase your productivity so that you won’t have to read all semester's work in less than three hours.

Failing to plan is planning to fail — a form of procrastination you should address. Planning allows you to get things done efficiently and helps you remember things that you need to do. When you do not plan, you rely on your memory because planning takes time, and you prefer to play catch up.

Make your priorities known and follow them diligently using a project planning guide, which you can download online. This guide will help you navigate your To-Dos in their order of importance. Attend to them without leaving any box unchecked. Planning cuts across different aspects of your life. The key ingredient to effective planning is to enter your activities and commitment in a scheduled paper planner. Note that writing in a paper planner, which is time- and cost-efficient, helps you remember.

chapters.title: Creating a routine and a ritual makes getting rid of procrastination easy
chapters.text:
A routine will help you conquer procrastination. That way, you can easily fall into your course of repeated activities without having to stress or defer your tasks to a future date. When it comes to fighting procrastination, there is absolutely no one-size-fits-all. Following your routine and rituals can be one way to go, but actually doing the work still rests on you.

Routines can make life easier, save you time and provide a sense of calm and control. ~ Leslie Josel

Knowing the difference between routine and rituals will help you follow the proper steps.

• Routine is something you perform in a specific period of time.
• Ritual is the order or procedure you follow in working.
• These two work hand-in-hand in fighting procrastination.
• Organization plays a crucial role in getting rid of procrastination.

When all your things are in the proper space, you don’t have a problem searching and using them whenever you need them.

Getting organized also creates a motivating working environment and cuts across everything from your bedroom to your school supplies. Asking yourself the right questions can help you design the proper cycle instead of getting stuck in the procrastination loop. Consider the following questions when you feel you’re sliding into procrastination:

• What plan do I have to…(fill in the activity)? This question helps you create a schedule or routine that you can follow.
• What steps do I need to take to…? Answering this question helps you develop a mental checklist for getting a task done.
• What is the first thing to do to…? This question chunks the task into manageable bits that you can easily complete.
• What will I do after this current task? This knowledge will strengthen your sense of time.
• What are the possible distractions to my priorities today? The answer to this will help you clarify your priorities and develop a plan for anticipated distractions.
• What do I know? This is better than saying: “I don’t know? It helps you see the gap between the known and unknown so that you can find a way to fill it.

chapters.title: Avoid distractions at all costs to achieve worthwhile goals in life
chapters.text:
Everywhere you turn, there are different forms of distractions. The funny thing about distractions is they tend to catch us off guard, and they can also be impulsive — internally and externally. Since we don’t plan for them, they tend to make us procrastinate, which can be a serious issue.

The computer you use for work, even if it’s for school, can be a great source of distraction. But with proper planning, you can maximize the use of your computer and focus on what you need to get done without outside distraction.

Your environment plays a huge role in encouraging you to initiate and complete your work. If you don’t like where you spend your time, you’re not going to get down to business, no matter what that business is. ~ Leslie Josel

When trying to avoid distractions, comfortability is key. When comfortable in your own space, you can set up your plans and stick to them to assist you in managing your time effectively and getting rid of procrastination.

Research has shown that we sometimes procrastinate based on our current mood. Procrastinators often attempt to avoid anxiety, fear, or boredom brought on by challenging tasks. But there is the need to shake free of that fear or anxiety and stay motivated. It can be hard and a bit tricky, but it’s possible. When you allow anxiety to cause you to procrastinate, you’ve only deferred the anxiety to a later date because it will compound and return multiple folds.

Getting started can sometimes be overwhelming, but you can always begin with a simple task. Do not strive to achieve perfection. Instead, set up realistic expectations for yourself and standards that are achievable.

Seeking perfection can derail you and get you stuck, ultimately causing you to procrastinate constantly.

Did you know? Natural light increases productivity, creates less stress, and improves overall health.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Procrastination hinders your productivity and affects every aspect of your life. It is not something you can just get rid of in a twinkle of an eye. It takes work and dedication. Funny how you have to work to stop procrastinating.

Just because everyone procrastinates does not mean you have to make it a habit. To effectively manage your procrastination, you need to plan your daily activities properly. Constant and correct planning requires knowing what works for you and what doesn’t.

When you can visualize the consequences of not getting a task done, you gain the courage to overcome the reluctance to start a task. Usually, you’ll find that starting is the hardest part.

Avoid obvious distractions and stay motivated. Using digestible tips and strategies found in this excellent guide will allow you to manage procrastination and stay productive.

Proper time management, creating doable schedules, and getting the right motivation are vital to procrastination management. Make easy-to-follow routines and rituals that will guide your daily activities. Strive to create an environment that makes you happy and motivates you to complete your tasks.

Procrastination can sometimes control you and your decision-making. Take back that control by redesigning your environment to become your ally and not your foe. Remember that being organized goes a long way in making procrastination difficult. It also saves you the time spent searching for things you need.

Regaining control of your life will have a tremendous impact on your mental health and general well-being. You’ll begin to see meaningful contributions that will affect the lives of others positively. Ultimately, you will live without regrets and die knowing that you gave your best in life.

If you get stuck, get away from your desk. Take a walk, take a bath, go to sleep, make a pie, draw, listen to ­music, meditate, exercise; whatever you do, don't just stick there scowling at the problem. ~ Hilary Mantel

Try this

Trying to get rid of procrastination can be tasking initially, but it becomes easy once you can navigate the basics and understand why you procrastinate in the first place. To do this, use the tips below:
• Make appointments with yourself — that way, you can be motivated and therefore get rid of procrastination.
• Know how to manage your time effectively time management helps focus.
• Planning is crucial; plan your day to the last minute and follow the plans diligently.
• Design your environment to motivate you to get things done.
